intro: Migrating from TypeScript to R transitions software development from a general-purpose, web-centric runtime structure to a specialized data analysis and statistical environment. Developers adopt R to run heavy data science computation pipelines, mathematical transformations, and native data visualizations without writing custom math logic. This transition replaces single-threaded event architectures and object models with 1-indexed vectorized calculations and native Data Frame transformations.

diffs.from: Static structural validation layer compiled away at build phases, operating over fluid JavaScript object shapes.
diffs.to: Dynamic type system built entirely around vector collections, data frames, matrices, factors, and nominal S3/S4 object hierarchies.

diffs.from: C-style syntax reliant on brackets, explicit semicolons, arrow assignments, and clear modular imports.
diffs.to: Functional, mathematically oriented layout utilizing arrow assignments (<-), custom formula indicators (~), and implicit method returns.

diffs.from: Volatile object graph tracking overseen automatically by generational garbage collection routines.
diffs.to: In-memory database constraints requiring entire active data blocks to sit within system RAM, managed via copy-on-write allocation schemes.

diffs.from: Single-thread non-blocking task execution driven natively by microtask queues and Promise workflows.
diffs.to: Synchronous, single-threaded execution model; parallel execution requires explicit cluster orchestration libraries like 'future'.

diffs.from: Sparse core package environment heavily dependent on NPM public packages for basic operational tooling.
diffs.to: Extensive statistical core, providing native linear regression, data grouping, array matrices, and statistical plotting tools out of the box.

examples.title: Vectorized Processing Operations
examples.description: Converting a TypeScript loop or map array transformation into a high-performance native vectorized matrix operation in R.
examples.fromCode:
const inputs: number[] = [10, 20, 30, 40];
const outputs = inputs.map(v => (v * 1.05) + 2);

examples.title: Data Filtering and Selection Primitives
examples.description: Filtering a dictionary array collection compared to filtering structural rows within an R Data Frame.
examples.toCode:
# Declaring an R Data Frame matrix
log <- data.frame(
  sku = c("A", "B"),
  stock = c(5, 12)
)
active <- subset(log, stock > 10)

tips.title: Remember 1-Based Structural Indexing
tips.body: R vectors and data matrices begin indexing paths at 1 rather than 0. Keep this shift in mind to avoid off-by-one errors when converting loop counters.

tips.title: Ditch Manual Loops for Vectorization
tips.body: Do not write 'for' statements to modify dataset elements. R performs operations across whole vectors simultaneously, which is much faster than index loops.

tips.title: Adopt Arrow Variables For Clean Scope
tips.body: While equal signs (=) work for variable assignment in many R environments, use the arrow operator (<-) to maintain clean scoping boundaries.

tips.title: Leverage Tidyverse for Array Pipeline Flow
tips.body: If you miss fluent TypeScript chaining methods (.filter().map()), import the 'tidyverse' package to use the pipe operator (%>%) for clear data staging.

faqs.question: What stands in for TypeScript null and undefined inside R datasets?
faqs.answer: R models missing or undefined data points using specialized values: 'NA' for unrecorded variables, and 'NaN' for undefined mathematical outputs.

faqs.question: Can I validate R calculations using static type linter configurations?
faqs.answer: R is fundamentally dynamic. You cannot apply ahead-of-time compile checks, but you can build assertions or runtime constraints using the 'types' package.

faqs.question: How do I serve R analytics models within production web backends?
faqs.answer: Wrap your R analytics pipeline inside a library like 'Plumber' to expose data outputs as public REST API routes that web apps can query.
